sentences.text: No.
These were the, the Jewish policemen, the Jewish kapo-- the, the Jewish policemen in the ghetto, yeah.
Well, no, not the kapo, not yet, not in the ghetto, no.
There were not kapos.
There were just policemen, yeah.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was a lot of hunger.
There was a lot of people who were very hungry.
I remember my cousin came to the house.
I don't remember her name-- or was it acquaintance?
And she was all swollen from hunger.
It was just impossible.
People were eating anything.
They made pancakes from grinds of coffee, grinds from beets, leaves, anything they could.
It was really very bad.
People were really starving and hungry.
Whatever little we had they cooked.
I don't remember.
My mother used to cook.
I was too young to--
There was a stove and--
--yeah.
So going back to the family that we lived with--
They were very mean to us.
Because when my mother-- when-- after they finished their cooking, they would put water on the-- on the fire.
And my mother had to start it from scratch.
It was really very bad.
So after a few more times when they came-- and the next time, my mother gave them some rice, which my father was-- my uncle from America-- my uncle left to America in 1939.
And he sent us two packages to the ghetto.
And because my father worked at-- there, he was able to get those packages.
And in one was some cocoa, some rice, even some butter, which was fresh.
And really, this, this-- my mother used it so sparingly, that we had it for a long time.
So once she gave them some rice, and they left again.
But it got to be so very often that we, we were just exhausted.
We were just-- the war of nerves was worse than anything else.
We couldn't stay there anymore, between the Appells and people looking for me.
We had an aunt that lived on the other side of the ghetto.
The ghetto was divided by a bridge.
And this aunt said, OK, you can come, you and your mom.
I mean, to my mother, she says, you and your daughter can come and stay with us, even though she knew that if she would have been-- if they would find us there, she would have been executed.
But she took us to her house.
And we stayed there with them.
And during the day, we used to hide.
We used to go to the cemetery, sleep there.
Or we used to hide someplace in the attic.
And one day when we came back, sure enough, the door was open.
And the Germans were there looking for us.
And--
Now it was the Germans looking for you, no longer--
There was the German that time.
They were coming.
Not the Jewish police, this was the German.
And--
No, not in a ghetto.
No, not in the ghetto.
Well, they all became, became race Deutschen.
All of a sudden, the Polish became German.
I don't know, yeah.
And where was I?
Oh, we were in my, my-- Aunt?
--aunt's house.
And we used to have ration breads.
And I had a little cousin.
And he always used to run, and he used to go and bring the bread, stay in line.
He used to organize-- bring some food, some vegetables-- what he used to bring.
A little boy-- he was maybe five or six.
So my mom said to me, well-- one day she said to me, why don't you go and stay in line and you bring the bread?
So I took the cards-- the rations.
And I went.
It was about couple of blocks over.
And I stood in line.
There was a big line.
And we were waiting for the bakery to open.
And the people were saying, oh, it doesn't matter.
The other day was also a very big line.
But then the German came.
And, and everything was in order.
And everybody got a bread.
But then, all of a sudden, I saw-- from far away, I saw some tanks coming on.
And I had a very bad feelings.
And I said to myself, Eva, you better get, get away from here.
And I ran away from the line.
And I went to a house that was right around the corner.
And when I walked into the house, there was a lot of people there hiding.
And I said to myself, this is no good, too many people.
And as I glanced over the-- across the street, I saw there was a little house with also a little garden.
And I didn't think twice.
I went to that garden.
And lucky for me, there was beets again.
[CRYING] And I lay down between the, the two, you know-- I don't know how you call those--
The rows of beet plants.
And I laid down, and I covered myself.
And there was another woman with a little boy.
And she saw me doing this.
She did the same thing.
And she laid down not far from me.
And it didn't take maybe two minutes that you heard the Germans came and started grabbing people, putting on those trucks.
And in that building that I was, you could hear the scream, [NON-ENGLISH]!
And people crying--
--and screaming. 'Listen, God!' They usually say that before they, they die.
And this is the prayers.
And the people were taken all from that building.
Every one of them were taken in trucks and taken away.
And one, one German, a SS man I saw, went to the house when I was hiding there, jumped over the, the, the vegetable things.
And he didn't see us.
And he went out.
And I was just praying to God.
I said, God, I, I don't mind.
But I want to be with my mother.
[SOBBING] If I die, I should be with her.
I didn't want to be by myself.
[SOBBING] And I prayed to God.
And I was sitting there.
And after a little while, it got quiet.
The trucks went away.
And I was laying there.
After about an hour or so, when it was very quiet, I heard some steps.
And I looked out from the vegetables garden.
And there was a man.
It was a policeman that lived in this house.
That would-- accounted for that he still had vegetables in his garden because most people didn't have anything.
And I asked him, is it OK to go home?
And he says, no, you stay there.
So I stood there.
And sure enough, another half an hour, when people started coming out, the Germans came back again and grabbed all the people from the street.
When it was getting really dark, he came.
And he says to me, OK, now you can go home.
The policeman-- the Jewish policeman.
She, she run away.
She went away before.
I don't know what happened to her.
But she had really me to thank because I gave her the idea of hiding in-between the vegetables.
Yes, they were.
They were beautiful.
Because the Polish earth was very rich, you know, the vegetables-- you don't see vegetables and, and fruit the way you saw in Poland.
They were really very, very beautiful, fruitful, very delicious, too.
They really-- you don't see tomatoes that tasted like-- I don't know whether Poland has the same thing now.
But that time, they did.
Not even New Jersey.
No, that was really-- so I was running home.
And when I came home, everybody was crying because they thought I was already gone.
Except my mom-- she started crying when she saw me.
And my little cousin was there, too.
He was also crying.
Everybody thought I was gone, that they, they took me away from, from the street.
But I was lucky once again.
This was-- this must have been maybe in August because we were another month.
And we couldn't stand it anymore, the daily, you know-- it was terrible.
In-- this was in '44, 44.
Three, three years, three and-- three years, I think, from--
Oh, this was--
This was in, in that-- in that place where they put my name on the list.
That's was-- there was-- I was pulling-- I don't know, those little--
--threads from, from the garments.
So it's really not, you know-- they just wanted to keep us occupied.
So we decided that we can't stay in the ghetto anymore.
So my aunt and my little cousin and me and my mom, we decided to give ourselves up.
Now, this was in September.
And we went to the train station.
And there was-- I think was already from the last people.
Because there was only a handful of people that still remained in the ghetto.
So they put us on the trains.
They put us on the trains.
They stuffed us like animals.
There was no room to breathe.
One was on top of the other.
People was dying of thirst and screaming and yelling.
And there was rain raining.
And I had a little-- a little cup.
So I put it out.
And I took some rainwater, and I gave it to my mom.
And I gave it to some people around.
I don't know how hygienic this was, but it helped a lot of people.
Fun?
Well, I used to read a lot.
But it was not compulsory, you know.
I used to be an avid reader.
I used to go to-- I don't remember where I got the books.
But I did read a lot.
But there wasn't-- it-- not really.
After the school ended and we went to work, it was just a day-to-day survival.
It was just hunger and fear.
I don't recall any.
Maybe some people that were religious, they still were.
But where I was, I don't remember anybody that was really religious.
No, there was no place where-- to go.
I mean, who-- when-- I remember-- this was when the war broke out.
I saw the Germans grab a Jew by the beard and tear-- tearing it off.
It was a horrible sight that we saw.
They took little kids, and they would knock their heads against the wall.
I mean, even if you were religious, you had to hide it.
You couldn't show it because your life was in danger.
You, you couldn't.
Yeah.
Yeah, this is the incident that occurred--
This was before the ghetto, right, what I just told you about the beard being pulled of this, this Jew's-- you know, the whole beard from his face.
It was--
Just grabbed and pulled him.
It was-- it was just too horrible to remember to think.
That's why I, like, distanced myself.
Like, I--
No, I was lucky.
I really was.
I was never hit.
I never--
Yeah, we were on a train.
And we were on a train for a long time.
And then we arrived to Auschwitz.
And we looked out.
And I saw a lot of people running around with stripes uniforms.
I says, well, what's going on here?
They looked like they-- from some kind of inmates [LAUGHS] or something.
So we went to-- they told us-- they opened the doors.
And they told everybody to get off.
So we got off the train.
And I was with my mother, my aunt, and that little cousin, and, I don't know, some neighbors or something.
I don't know.
But going halfway through, I told my mom, you know, Mom, I think we left the passport on the train.
And I left a little pot-- a little-- you know, a little pot because we won't have where to cook something.
So my mom went with me back to the train.
And my aunt and my little cousin went with the rest the people.
And that's the last we saw of them because they-- because of my little cousin was so young, my aunt went with him.
And when I came with my mom, we, we went-- we didn't see anymore-- I saw my other aunt.
There was my other aunt from my father's side-- was with a little tiny baby also.
And she also went with the little baby.
They all went.
We didn't know where we-- where they went.
But they went to one side.
And we went to another side.
They were all burned.
Because this was one more-- the last day that they were allowed to burn, when we came to Auschwitz in, in '44.
Because there was-- I don't know.
There was some kind of a-- the German got from, from Berlin-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they told him no more burning.
I don't know what the reason was.
But it was no-- in Auschwitz.
But we didn't know about that.
So me and my mom, we were looking for them.
But we, we couldn't find them.
So we just went and decided we were supposed to walk.
And my mom, because of this laying on that-- on that cement floor developed some kind of a-- I don't know how you call it in English, but--
[SPEAKING POLISH].
That means inflammation of the joints.
And so she, she was walking with a cane.
And as we were walking, there was one German that came over and called me and my mother.
Because I was limping.
I was born with-- I had polio when I was a little girl.
So I was limping, and my mother was walking with a cane.
So he called us out from the line.
And he says, you stay on the side here.
So my mother said, OK.
So I said to my mom, [SOBBING] Mom, I really don't want to die.
I'm so young.
And it was so beautiful, you know.
The, the scenery was breathtaking there in Auschwitz-- How old were you--
--and green.
I was 11.
[SOBBING] And I said, I really didn't live any.
I would like to live a little longer.
And my mother says, don't cry, my child.
She says, we should be the victims for all the people-- [SOBBING] [INAUDIBLE] around here.
If we should die-- in order the others should live.
And we were staying there.
And everybody was almost gone.
We were staying, too.
And there was a couple of officers that came over.
And he comes and asked my mother, what are you staying here?
And my mother says, the other officer told us-- in perfect German, she spoke perfect German.
She says, the other officer told us to stay on the side.
So he looks at me and looks at me and looks at my mother.
And I guess, you know, it must have been my eyes, they were so blue.
And I, I really-- You were a very beautiful child.
I was-- yeah.
And he says to me, go in [NON-ENGLISH].
So we went.
And when we came, they-- all the people were so happy to see us because they were sure that we were-- we were already sent to the oven.
And sure enough, my, my aunt with my little cousin, Judith, was sent to the oven.
And then they took us.
And they said we go in in [NON-ENGLISH]-- into the bath.
And we had to get undressed.
And my mother still had that little bundle of the jewelry with her.
And she was going to give it away.
And I said, no, Ma, I don't want to give them nothing.
So I made a little hole in the ground.
And I buried it.
I wanted-- really, I wanted to take-- she had one big diamond, to hide it.
My mother says, enough.
I don't want anything anymore.
This way, we're not going to live in fear.
So I took all this thing, and I buried it.
Probably somebody already got it after war.
They knew a lot of people did it.
So they took us, and they shaved us all around.
And I was standing next to my mom.
And I didn't even know her.
I says, Ma?
And she says, yeah, that's me.
And we went into the bath.
And luckily, there was not gas that came out, but water.
And after the bath, we went out.
And they gave us clothes.
They didn't look what size.
What-- you got a pair of shoes.
And they threw you a dress.
And they threw you a coat.
And they threw-- so we got dressed.
And I got a pair of shoes with heels like this.
And I, [LAUGHS] I didn't know what to do.
I couldn't wear it.
So my mother had her shoes that she had had from the ghetto that, you know-- like, very soft because she-- well, she had trouble.
And this woman, the-- this was a, a kapo.
She was, I think, a Czech-- Czechoslovakian kapo.
She took her shoes.
And she threw it down the hill.
And when I saw this, I didn't even think twice.
I ran down the hill and grabbed the shoes.
I knew my mother won't be able to walk without them.
And I grabbed it.
And I really risked my life because if she saw me-- but I got her back the shoes.
And people are dying of thirst.
It was such a hot day.
And everybody was so thirsty.
And they did-- they took the water hose.
And she just spilled the water next to the people.
But she wouldn't give anybody just to, you know, to make us miserable.
Yes, she was a Jewish-- from, I think, Czechoslovakia.
There was a few Czechs and a few Hungarian kapos.
There were some Jewish-- Polish Jews, too.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then they got us together.
We were in Auschwitz.
And they said we have to-- after the bath, when we have already stuff-- and we were going to the next town, which was Birkenau.
So we walked over there.
And I heard somebody say to us, oh, you are lucky you are not going there where the dust is.
So I said, see, Ma, we're not going to work in the streets when it's all the dust.
We're going to probably work in a factory where it's going to be clean.
I didn't know what they meant-- where it's-- where it's the dust.
And they meant we are going to get burned and turned into dust.
We didn't know that.
So we walked to Birkenau.
And we stayed there overnight, slept maybe 500 women in one little cubbyhole.
And the next morning, they told us to stay in line.
And we stood in line.
And there were train coming on.
And I was next to my mom.
And they cut through the lines.
One went to one train, and one to the other train.
I-- immediately, I stood behind my mother, so I should be with her together.
And we went on a train.
And we started our journey to a working camp, which happened to be in-- near Hamburg.
The name of it was Poppenbuttel-Sasel.
We went to this camp.
And they gave us barracks, you know.
Like, we were, I think, 16 women in one barrack.
It was like bunk beds.
And me and my mom got the lower bunk beds.
[SIGHS] The barrack was small, but there was a lot of barracks.
There was two big ones.
And there was a number of small one.
Once-- ours was a small one.
And we stayed in this barrack with, with those 16 other women, which, fortunately, quite a number of them are still alive.
Yeah, we used to be.
After the war, we're still in contact with my friends, yes.
Yeah, we were together, yeah.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I also forgot to tell you-- while in Auschwitz, when we had to go to the bathroom, we were all staying in groups of five.
You couldn't go to the bathroom when you wanted to.
It's when they told you have to go.
And if somebody was in the middle or nothing and they said, this is finished, they had to get out.
And I also forget to tell you yet-- and this was this one day in that Auschwitz.
When we came with my mom, we were staying in the barracks.
And this one kapo saw my mother with a cane.
And she grabbed the cane.
And she says, I'll be-- you'll be better off without it.
She grabbed the cane.
And unfortunately, she, she broke this cane on another poor woman's back when she couldn't hold-- she had diarrhea.
It was terrible.
It was just unbelievable what we went through.
I mean, you can't imagine.
In the camps?
Well, we got, I think, one soup-- a thin soup once a day, I remember.
But in the working camp, we also got a bread for, for the 16 women--
--yeah.
Oh, every morning, they would send us in trains to Hamburg.
So we had to run to catch the train.
And it was a big walk.
And it was hard for my mother.
So whenever I could, I would volunteer I should go for her.
But sometimes, even she-- they wouldn't-- she wouldn't even have to go.
But I volunteered just to make sure that they don't take her.
So we used to run.
And we used to go into Hamburg.
And we were staying by bombed-out houses.
And what we did, we stayed in a line.
Like, one would give a brick to the other.
It was just-- it was not really work.
This was something to, to make us miserable, you know.
So we-- one would give the brick to the other.
And we made that-- a big thing.
And overnight-- we came next day.
The house was bombed again.
And we started anew.
It was just, just to make us miserable.
And then one day, they took us to a different place.
They, they said we should build something.
And I had-- I, I took a mortar and a brick.
And I was being a mason.
And-- but most of the time, I really was hiding.
My mother said, why don't you work?
You're going to get warm.
Because it was very cold.
And some people took the cement bags and put on that bag to keep warm.
But when the Germans saw, they would really hit them very severely.
It was winter, yeah.
And I was once hiding in a cement-- in a cement machine.
And my mom says, [LAUGHS] you're going to get killed.
I said, no, [LAUGHS] I'm going to be warm there.
And I was hiding in that cement-- yeah.
And this was in that working camp.
And every day, we used to come.
And they used to start counting us.
And we would stay for hours.
And they would count, and they would count, and they would count.
And it was just miserable.
Because it wasn't the time yet.
They, they-- there was still-- the war was still on.
They, they thought maybe, you know, they didn't want to kill everybody at once.
I don't know why.
But we had our-- [LAUGHS] the guy who was in charge of the camp was a very educated German.
He spoke Hebrew fluently.
And he used to have music.
When we used to come from work, there was music there.
No cocktails.
Yeah.
No, just music.
I don't remember.
But there was a woman here that, that survived that he really liked-- this woman.
And he told her, if they're ever going to dispense this camp, run and hide.
I guess she must have been.
I don't know.
I don't know.
Some people were lucky that, you know, that they got a job in the kitchen.
So they had a little bit more soup.
They, see, like we-- I was never lucky I like this.
Once me and my girlfriend went to steal the kohlrabi, you know.
And I was holding her up.
[LAUGHS] And she stole from the kitchen.
And we brought it-- once, we were almost caught by a German.
I heard the steps.
And I said to her, let's go, Helen.
So I dropped her, and I ran.
And then she ran after me.
And-- but we had that, that, that big vegetable.
So we had it for a few days.
And then we-- I also forgot to tell you.
There, we were very thirsty.
And there was nothing to drink in the-- in the camp that time.
And my mom said, she's got to drink.
So she said-- there was a bathroom.
And the water was running.
And she said, she's going to drink the water from the bathroom.
And I said, Ma, you're not going to do it.
So she saw some puddle of water that was green.
She wants to drink.
I said, Ma, no, please.
And I was walking by.
And I saw there-- two women while holding a pail with water going to the kitchen.
And I took out the little pot that I had.
And I put it in.
And I got some for my mother.
I said, see, I didn't want you to drink this.
God didn't want you to get sick from that water that was from, from, from the latrine.
[LAUGHS] I don't know.
I don't know.
And also, in that working camp, once a week we used to get soup-- a thick soup.
That was once a month.
It was a rice soup with milk.
And I said to my mom, Mom, after the war, every day you're going to cook me this soup.
Because I, I had a strange belief all the-- through the war that we're going to survive.
I just had a feeling that we're going to survive.
And-- yeah.
And I--
No.
[LAUGHS]
She was right.
She was-- no, she was right.
I never did.
And there was a girl that used to cut our bread very, very evenly.
This girl I met after the war in the street.
I recognized her.
She's here in the United States, too.
Well, we were--
What happened next-- when the German heard that they were beginning to lose-- oh, no I forgot to tell you one-- also things.
We were working-- we went to a place that was like a-- Rinderhalle.
It was like a slaughterhouse that-- we worked there.
And I don't know whether I should tell you about the incidents with the woman.
Well, everybody of us had a coat.
And inside was cut out a piece of material.
And everybody had a different color, like, in the back of their coats.
And we were in the Rinderhalle.
And there was a lot of cows and bulls.
And I said to my mom, Mom, I'm going to-- of course, with my little pot-- I said, I'm going to go.
And I'm going to get some milk, you know.
I'm going to go start-- I, I know from milking a cow, like, [LAUGHS] you know.
But I went.
And we went.
And another-- it was a few other women that went.
And we went over there.
And I was looking for a cow to milk.
And there was another woman.
And she was next to a bull.
And she had the, the misfortune of having a red piece of cloth in her back of her coat.
And when she went to the cow next to the bull, of course, they're very protective-- must have been his mate or something.
And he charged at this woman.
And he-- you know, she opened her mouth to scream.
And the bull just tore up her face.
And I was next to that.
And I started screaming.
And I was running through the-- through the gates.
I don't know what strength-- God must have given me the strength to run over the big gates.
And I was-- started running.
Please, help!
Help!
A woman is being hurt.
And she's terribly-- she's, she's in trouble.
And I ran and ran and jumped.
And finally, the people came.
And they pulled away this, this, this bull from her.
But it was a terrible experience.
I never forgot.
I don't know how, how I could run away.
The woman survived.
But the face was all with pus.
And I don't know whether she survived the war.
But immediately, she did survive.
And after this, well, we went there.
We were once sitting.
And we, I don't know, we were sitting there, and-- by work.
And a German came over there.
And he comes to my mother.
And he says, is it true that you are all prostitutes?
And my mother says-- I'm going to interrupt now.
OK.
OK.
OK, fine.
